insight_panel.title: Algorithm Insight
insight_panel.noData: No additional insight available for this algorithm yet.

bubbleSort.history: Bubble Sort is one of the earliest and most elementary sorting algorithms. The term was popularized by Iverson in 1962, though similar algorithms appeared in the 1950s. Despite its simplicity, it served as the baseline comparison for decades of sorting research.
bubbleSort.intuition: Imagine repeatedly walking through a list of numbers. Whenever you find two adjacent numbers that are in the wrong order, you swap them. After each full pass, the largest unsorted number 'bubbles up' to its correct position at the end. Repeat until no more swaps are needed.
bubbleSort.realWorldUses_0: Teaching and academic demonstrations of sorting concepts
bubbleSort.realWorldUses_1: Detecting when a nearly-sorted list is already in order (early termination variant)
bubbleSort.realWorldUses_2: Graphics pipelines for sorting a very small number of elements (polygon depth ordering)
bubbleSort.facts_0: The name 'Bubble Sort' was formally coined by Kenneth Iverson in his 1962 book A Programming Language.
bubbleSort.facts_1: Despite its O(n²) average case, it performs optimally O(n) on already-sorted input with the early-exit variant.
bubbleSort.facts_2: Donald Knuth described it as 'not recommended for most purposes' in The Art of Computer Programming, yet it remains one of the most widely taught algorithms.

quickSort.history: Invented by Tony Hoare in 1959 while he was a visiting student at Moscow State University working on machine translation. He needed to sort words for a Russian-English dictionary project. The algorithm was published in 1961 and remained one of the fastest practical sorting algorithms for decades.
quickSort.intuition: Pick one element as a 'pivot'. Rearrange the other elements so that everything smaller than the pivot goes to the left, and everything larger goes to the right. Now the pivot is in its final correct position. Recursively apply the same idea to the left and right sub-arrays. Divide and conquer!
quickSort.realWorldUses_0: Default sort in many standard libraries (C's qsort, early Java Arrays.sort)
quickSort.realWorldUses_1: Database engines for in-memory sorting of query result sets
quickSort.realWorldUses_2: Operating system kernels for scheduling and memory management structures
quickSort.realWorldUses_3: Competitive programming as a go-to general-purpose sort
quickSort.facts_0: Tony Hoare invented Quick Sort at age 25. He later won the Turing Award in 1980.
quickSort.facts_1: The average case O(n log n) performance comes from the pivot splitting the array roughly in half each time.
quickSort.facts_2: The worst case O(n²) occurs when the pivot is always the smallest or largest element. In practice this is solved with random pivot selection.
quickSort.facts_3: Quick Sort is not stable: equal elements may appear in a different relative order after sorting.

mergeSort.history: Merge Sort was conceived by John von Neumann in 1945, making it one of the oldest divide-and-conquer algorithms. It was originally designed for efficient use on early magnetic tape storage systems, where sequential access was the only efficient operation. Its formal description was published by John von Neumann and Herman Goldstine.
mergeSort.intuition: Split the array in half, sort each half independently (recursively), then merge the two sorted halves into one sorted result. Merging two already-sorted lists is easy: just compare the front of each list and pick the smaller one. The key insight is that merging is O(n) and the recursion depth is O(log n).
mergeSort.realWorldUses_0: Java's Arrays.sort for objects (uses TimSort, a merge sort hybrid)
mergeSort.realWorldUses_1: External sorting of files too large to fit in memory (merge chunks from disk)
mergeSort.realWorldUses_2: Counting inversions in an array, a fundamental task in data analysis
mergeSort.realWorldUses_3: Parallel sorting in distributed systems (each node sorts a chunk, then chunks are merged)
mergeSort.facts_0: Merge Sort was one of the first algorithms designed specifically around the constraints of tape storage hardware.
mergeSort.facts_1: It is the only simple O(n log n) algorithm that is also stable (preserves the order of equal elements).
mergeSort.facts_2: John von Neumann is also known for the von Neumann architecture, the design most modern computers are built on.
mergeSort.facts_3: TimSort, used by Python and Java, is a hybrid of Merge Sort and Insertion Sort optimized for real-world partially sorted data.

insertionSort.history: Insertion Sort is an ancient sorting technique that mirrors how people naturally sort playing cards in their hands. It has been used informally for centuries and formalized in computer science in the 1950s. Its simplicity and efficiency on small or nearly sorted input makes it a cornerstone of hybrid algorithms.
insertionSort.intuition: Think of how you would sort a hand of playing cards. You pick up cards one at a time and insert each new card into its correct position among the cards already sorted in your hand. In the computer version, you scan left until you find the right spot, shifting elements along the way.
insertionSort.realWorldUses_0: Used as the base case for small sub-arrays inside Tim Sort and Intro Sort
insertionSort.realWorldUses_1: Sorting real-time incoming data streams where elements arrive one at a time
insertionSort.realWorldUses_2: Embedded systems with very limited memory where code simplicity matters
insertionSort.facts_0: Insertion Sort is optimal for input sizes under about 10–20 elements, which is why hybrid algorithms use it for small partitions.
insertionSort.facts_1: It is an online algorithm: it can sort a list as it receives items without needing all input upfront.
insertionSort.facts_2: The number of swaps equals the number of inversions in the input, a useful measure in combinatorics.

selectionSort.history: Selection Sort is one of the most intuitive sorting algorithms, using a strategy that predates computers. It works by repeatedly finding the minimum remaining element and placing it in its correct position.
selectionSort.intuition: Divide the array into a sorted region (on the left) and an unsorted region. Scan the unsorted region for the smallest element, swap it with the first unsorted element, expanding the sorted region by one. Repeat until the entire array is sorted.
selectionSort.realWorldUses_0: Situations where the number of writes must be minimized (flash memory, EEPROM)
selectionSort.realWorldUses_1: Teaching environments where simplicity is valued over performance
selectionSort.realWorldUses_2: Small embedded systems where code size and predictable behavior are critical
selectionSort.facts_0: Selection Sort always performs exactly n(n-1)/2 comparisons regardless of input order. It is not adaptive.
selectionSort.facts_1: It makes at most n-1 swaps, giving it an advantage over Bubble Sort on write-expensive storage.
selectionSort.facts_2: Selection Sort is not stable in its standard form.

heapSort.history: Heap Sort was invented by J. W. J. Williams in 1964, who also introduced the heap data structure in the same paper. Robert Floyd later improved it with a more efficient heap-building phase, reducing build time to O(n).
heapSort.intuition: A max-heap is a tree where every parent is larger than its children. First, rearrange the array into a max-heap. Then repeatedly extract the largest element (the root), place it at the end of the array, and restore the heap property. The result is a sorted array.
heapSort.realWorldUses_0: Priority queues in operating system schedulers
heapSort.realWorldUses_1: Graph algorithms like Dijkstra and Prim that require efficient minimum/maximum extraction
heapSort.realWorldUses_2: Embedded real-time systems that need guaranteed worst-case O(n log n) performance
heapSort.facts_0: Heap Sort is in-place and achieves O(n log n) worst case, something Quick Sort cannot guarantee.
heapSort.facts_1: The heap data structure invented for Heap Sort is the foundation of priority queues used everywhere in computing.
heapSort.facts_2: Despite its theoretical optimality, Heap Sort is rarely the fastest in practice due to poor cache locality.

shellSort.history: Shell Sort was invented by Donald Shell in 1959 and published in Communications of the ACM. It was one of the first algorithms to break the O(n²) barrier for general-purpose sorting, even without a formal proof at the time.
shellSort.intuition: Shell Sort is a generalization of Insertion Sort. Instead of comparing adjacent elements, it compares elements separated by a gap. The gap starts large (sorting elements far apart) and gradually shrinks to 1 (a final Insertion Sort pass). Because elements are moved over large distances early on, the final passes are very efficient.
shellSort.realWorldUses_0: Embedded systems where small code size matters but O(n²) is too slow
shellSort.realWorldUses_1: Sorting medium-sized datasets where a simple implementation is preferred
shellSort.realWorldUses_2: Linux kernel's uClibc library uses Shell Sort for its simplicity and reasonable performance
shellSort.facts_0: The optimal gap sequence for Shell Sort is still an open research problem in computer science.
shellSort.facts_1: With Knuth's gap sequence (1, 4, 13, 40, ...) the average case is approximately O(n^1.5).
shellSort.facts_2: Donald Shell published the algorithm at age 29; it was one of the landmark algorithm papers of the era.

radixSort.history: Radix Sort can be traced back to 1887 when Herman Hollerith used it on punched card tabulating machines. It was later formalized for computers in the 1950s. The non-comparative nature makes it theoretically faster than comparison-based sorts for integer data.
radixSort.intuition: Sort numbers digit by digit, starting from the least significant digit to the most significant. Use a stable sort (like Counting Sort) at each digit position. After processing all digit positions, the numbers are fully sorted. The key insight: sorting digit-by-digit in a stable way makes the whole sequence correct.
radixSort.realWorldUses_0: Sorting large arrays of integers or fixed-length strings in database systems
radixSort.realWorldUses_1: Network packet routing and IP address sorting
radixSort.realWorldUses_2: Suffix array construction in bioinformatics and text processing
radixSort.facts_0: Radix Sort is one of the oldest sorting algorithms, predating programmable computers.
radixSort.facts_1: Its linear time O(nk) is achieved without ever comparing two elements directly.
radixSort.facts_2: The algorithm works in both LSD (least significant digit first) and MSD (most significant digit first) variants.

countingSort.history: Counting Sort was described by Harold H. Seward in 1954. It works by counting occurrences of each value and using that information to place each element directly in its correct sorted position. That is a radically different approach from comparison-based sorting.
countingSort.intuition: Count how many times each value appears in the input. Then compute the cumulative counts to determine where each value's sorted block starts. Finally, place each element in its correct output position. There are no comparisons between elements, only arithmetic on counts.
countingSort.realWorldUses_0: Sorting characters or small integers in text processing pipelines
countingSort.realWorldUses_1: Used as a subroutine inside Radix Sort for each digit position
countingSort.realWorldUses_2: Histogramming and frequency analysis in image processing
countingSort.facts_0: Counting Sort achieves linear O(n + k) time, better than any comparison-based sort for small ranges.
countingSort.facts_1: It is a stable sort, which is essential for its use inside Radix Sort.
countingSort.facts_2: The algorithm requires O(k) extra space for the counting array, making it impractical when k >> n.

bucketSort.history: Bucket Sort is a distribution-based sorting algorithm whose conceptual origins trace back to the early days of computing. It distributes elements into buckets, sorts each bucket, and concatenates the results.
bucketSort.intuition: Imagine distributing mail into labeled bins by region. Each bin (bucket) contains mail for a specific range of addresses. You sort each bin independently (with a simple algorithm), then collect the bins in order. Bucket Sort applies the same idea to numbers distributed across a known range.
bucketSort.realWorldUses_0: Sorting floating-point numbers uniformly distributed in [0, 1)
bucketSort.realWorldUses_1: Organizing data in geographic information systems by coordinate ranges
bucketSort.realWorldUses_2: Efficient sorting in external memory when data can be partitioned by value range
bucketSort.facts_0: Bucket Sort achieves average O(n + k) time when input is uniformly distributed.
bucketSort.facts_1: The worst case degenerates to O(n²) if all elements fall into a single bucket.
bucketSort.facts_2: It is not a comparison-based sort. Elements are placed by value, not by pairwise comparison.

cycleSort.history: Cycle Sort was formalized by Brendan Kehoe around 1990. It is theoretically optimal for minimizing writes, making exactly n-1 write operations in the worst case, fewer than any other sorting algorithm.
cycleSort.intuition: Every permutation of elements can be decomposed into cycles. A cycle tells you: element A should go where B is, B should go where C is, ..., and the last element should go where A was. Cycle Sort traces each cycle and performs exactly the minimum number of writes needed to resolve it.
cycleSort.realWorldUses_0: Flash memory devices where writes are expensive and degrade the hardware
cycleSort.realWorldUses_1: EEPROM sorting where write cycles are limited
cycleSort.facts_0: Cycle Sort is the only sorting algorithm that is provably optimal in the number of writes.
cycleSort.facts_1: The number of writes is n minus the number of cycles in the permutation.
cycleSort.facts_2: Despite its unique property, Cycle Sort is rarely used in practice due to its O(n²) time complexity.

combSort.history: Comb Sort was invented by Włodzimierz Dobosiewicz in 1980 and later popularized by Stephen Lacey and Richard Box in a 1991 Byte Magazine article. It improves Bubble Sort by using a shrinking gap.
combSort.intuition: Comb Sort extends Bubble Sort with one key change: instead of comparing adjacent elements (gap = 1), start with a large gap and shrink it by a factor of 1.3 each pass. This allows small values near the end (called 'turtles') to be moved to the front quickly, which is Bubble Sort's key weakness. The final passes with gap = 1 finish the job.
combSort.realWorldUses_0: Simple in-place sorting where Bubble Sort's 'turtle' problem causes poor performance
combSort.realWorldUses_1: Embedded systems where a better-than-O(n²) average case is desired with minimal code
combSort.realWorldUses_2: Educational contexts to demonstrate gap-sequence-based improvements
combSort.facts_0: The shrink factor of ~1.3 was empirically determined to be near-optimal.
combSort.facts_1: Comb Sort eliminates the 'turtle problem' that makes Bubble Sort slow on certain inputs.
combSort.facts_2: The worst case remains O(n²), but average case performance is significantly better.

timSort.history: Tim Sort was designed and implemented by Tim Peters in 2002 for the Python programming language. It was engineered for real-world data, which is often partially sorted. Today it is the default sort in Python, Java (for objects), Swift, Android, and several other platforms.
timSort.intuition: Tim Sort looks for naturally ordered sub-sequences ('runs') in the input. If a run is too short, it extends it using Insertion Sort. Then it merges these runs using a merge strategy borrowed from Merge Sort, with careful bookkeeping to avoid redundant work on already-ordered data.
timSort.realWorldUses_0: Python's built-in sort() and sorted() functions
timSort.realWorldUses_1: Java's Arrays.sort() for objects and Collections.sort()
timSort.realWorldUses_3: Any real-world dataset with naturally occurring ordered sequences
timSort.facts_0: Tim Peters designed Tim Sort specifically because real-world data is rarely fully random.
timSort.facts_1: Tim Sort is both stable and adaptive. It is faster than O(n log n) on partially sorted input.
timSort.facts_2: It was formally proved correct and had its worst-case guarantees verified by academic researchers in 2015.
timSort.facts_3: The name 'Tim Sort' is informal; Tim Peters jokingly suggested it when the algorithm was being adopted in Java.

bogoSort.history: Bogo Sort (also called Permutation Sort or Stupid Sort) has no serious inventor or date of origin. It is a joke algorithm from computer science folklore, used to illustrate the absurdity of non-analytical approaches to problem solving.
bogoSort.intuition: Randomly shuffle the entire array. Check if it is sorted. If not, shuffle again. Repeat indefinitely. The algorithm eventually terminates with probability 1 (theoretically), but in practice it is astronomically unlikely to sort even a small array quickly.
bogoSort.realWorldUses_0: Classroom demonstrations of why algorithmic analysis matters
bogoSort.realWorldUses_1: Humorous examples in algorithm textbooks and lectures
bogoSort.facts_0: The average number of shuffles needed to sort n elements is n! (factorial), which grows faster than any polynomial or exponential.
bogoSort.facts_1: The worst case is technically unbounded. The algorithm might never terminate.
bogoSort.facts_2: Bogo Sort holds the distinction of being the least efficient sorting algorithm commonly discussed in academia.

bfs.history: Breadth-First Search was first described by Konrad Zuse in his 1945 thesis (though unpublished for years). Edward F. Moore independently rediscovered it in 1959 for finding shortest paths through mazes. C. Y. Lee later applied it to circuit routing in 1961. It has since become one of the foundational graph traversal algorithms.
bfs.intuition: Start at a source node. Explore all of its immediate neighbors first, then all of their neighbors, then their neighbors, expanding outward in concentric 'waves'. This level-by-level exploration guarantees that the first time you reach a destination, you have taken the shortest path (in terms of the number of edges).
bfs.realWorldUses_0: Finding the shortest path in unweighted networks (social graphs, routing)
bfs.realWorldUses_1: Web crawlers (crawling the web breadth-first from a starting URL)
bfs.realWorldUses_2: Level-order traversal of trees in compilers and XML parsers
bfs.realWorldUses_3: Peer-to-peer network broadcasting (Bitcoin, BitTorrent neighbor discovery)
bfs.facts_0: BFS guarantees the shortest path in unweighted graphs, a property not shared by DFS.
bfs.facts_1: The algorithm was reinvented multiple times independently, showing its naturalness and utility.
bfs.facts_2: BFS is the basis for many other algorithms including Dijkstra's algorithm (a weighted generalization).
bfs.facts_3: Its memory usage is O(V) in the worst case. It must store all nodes at the current frontier.

dijkstra.history: Edsger W. Dijkstra conceived this algorithm in 1956 and published it in 1959. He designed it in roughly 20 minutes while sitting in a café in Amsterdam, later calling it one of his most elegant creations. It was originally meant to demonstrate the capabilities of the ARMAC computer.
dijkstra.intuition: Maintain a set of visited nodes and a priority queue of tentative distances. Always expand the unvisited node with the smallest known distance. When you expand a node, check if going through it provides a shorter path to any of its neighbors. Repeat until the destination is reached.
dijkstra.realWorldUses_0: GPS and mapping software (Google Maps, Apple Maps route calculation)
dijkstra.realWorldUses_1: Internet routing protocols (OSPF, Open Shortest Path First)
dijkstra.realWorldUses_3: Robot motion planning in known environments
dijkstra.facts_0: Dijkstra designed the algorithm in ~20 minutes without pencil or paper.
dijkstra.facts_1: The algorithm fails with negative edge weights. Use Bellman-Ford in that case.
dijkstra.facts_2: Dijkstra received the Turing Award in 1972, one of the highest honors in computer science.
dijkstra.facts_3: With a Fibonacci heap, Dijkstra runs in O(E + V log V), optimal for dense graphs.

aStar.history: A* was developed in 1968 by Peter Hart, Nils Nilsson, and Bertram Raphael at the Stanford Research Institute as part of the Shakey robotics project, one of the first AI robots. They needed a general, efficient pathfinding algorithm that could guide the robot through physical space.
aStar.intuition: A* extends Dijkstra by adding a heuristic: an estimate of how far the current node is from the goal. For each node, compute f(n) = g(n) + h(n), where g(n) is the actual cost from start and h(n) is the estimated cost to the goal. Always expand the node with the smallest f(n). A good heuristic focuses the search toward the goal, skipping irrelevant areas.
aStar.realWorldUses_0: Video game pathfinding (virtually every commercial game uses A* or a variant)
aStar.realWorldUses_1: Robot navigation and autonomous vehicle path planning
aStar.realWorldUses_2: Map routing in navigation apps with turn penalties and traffic
aStar.realWorldUses_3: Puzzle solving (8-puzzle, 15-puzzle, planning problems in AI)
aStar.facts_0: A* is optimal and complete: it will always find the shortest path if one exists, given an admissible heuristic.
aStar.facts_1: The Manhattan distance heuristic is admissible for grid-based pathfinding without diagonals.
aStar.facts_2: A* degenerates to Dijkstra when h(n) = 0 and to Greedy Best-First Search when g(n) = 0.
aStar.facts_3: The Shakey robot project where A* was created was a landmark milestone in the history of artificial intelligence.

bidirectionalSearch.history: Bidirectional search was described by Pohl in 1969 as a way to dramatically reduce search space in large graphs. By running two simultaneous BFS searches, one from the start and one from the goal, the frontiers meet in the middle, covering roughly the square root of the total nodes.
bidirectionalSearch.intuition: Run two BFS searches simultaneously: one forward from the start and one backward from the goal. The searches expand level by level, each maintaining their own visited sets. When a node is discovered by both searches (the frontiers intersect), a complete path has been found by combining the two partial paths.
bidirectionalSearch.realWorldUses_0: Large social network shortest-path queries (e.g., six degrees of separation)
bidirectionalSearch.realWorldUses_1: Transportation route planning with known start and destination
bidirectionalSearch.realWorldUses_2: Game AI pathfinding in very large maps where single-direction search is too slow
bidirectionalSearch.facts_0: Bidirectional BFS explores O(b^(d/2)) nodes instead of O(b^d), exponentially fewer for deep searches.
bidirectionalSearch.facts_1: Guaranteeing the shortest path requires careful synchronization of when the frontiers meet.
bidirectionalSearch.facts_2: Bidirectional Dijkstra was used in the original Google Maps routing before being replaced by more sophisticated techniques.

greedyBestFirstSearch.history: Greedy Best-First Search is a simplification of A*. By ignoring the actual cost g(n) and expanding only based on the heuristic h(n), it prioritizes speed over optimality. The trade-off is that it may find a non-optimal path.
greedyBestFirstSearch.intuition: Always expand the node that looks closest to the goal according to your heuristic. Do not worry about how far you have already traveled; just focus on the estimated distance remaining. This greediness makes it fast, but it can lead you down dead ends or suboptimal paths.
greedyBestFirstSearch.realWorldUses_0: Real-time game AI where finding any path quickly is more valuable than the optimal path
greedyBestFirstSearch.realWorldUses_1: Navigation in large open environments where heuristic guidance is reliable
greedyBestFirstSearch.realWorldUses_2: Initial pathfinding pass in multi-stage planners where optimality is refined later
greedyBestFirstSearch.facts_0: Greedy Best-First Search is not guaranteed to find the shortest path.
greedyBestFirstSearch.facts_1: It can expand far fewer nodes than A* or Dijkstra when the heuristic is very accurate.
greedyBestFirstSearch.facts_2: With a perfect heuristic, Greedy Best-First Search goes directly to the goal without exploring irrelevant nodes.

jumpPointSearch.history: Jump Point Search was invented by Daniel Harabor and Alban Grastien and presented at AAAI 2011. It was designed specifically for uniform-cost grid maps, the standard representation in game navigation, and can be over an order of magnitude faster than A* on such maps.
jumpPointSearch.intuition: On a uniform-cost grid, many paths are symmetric (they have the same cost but different routes). Jump Point Search eliminates this symmetry by skipping straight ahead or diagonally until it reaches a 'jump point', a node that has forced neighbors not reachable by the straight-line path. Only jump points are added to the open set, drastically reducing the number of nodes examined.
jumpPointSearch.realWorldUses_2: Large-scale pathfinding with hundreds of simultaneous agents
jumpPointSearch.facts_0: JPS can be 10–100× faster than standard A* on open grid maps.
jumpPointSearch.facts_1: It only works correctly on uniform-cost grids (all move costs equal).
jumpPointSearch.facts_2: The key innovation is identifying and skipping symmetric paths, reducing the search space dramatically.

bellmanFord.history: The Bellman-Ford algorithm was developed by Richard Bellman and Lester Ford Jr. independently in 1956–1958. It solves the single-source shortest path problem for graphs with negative edge weights, a case where Dijkstra's algorithm fails.
bellmanFord.intuition: Relax all edges repeatedly. In each relaxation, update the distance estimate to a node if going through its neighbor gives a shorter path. After V-1 iterations (V = number of vertices), all shortest paths are found. If distances still change in a V-th iteration, the graph contains a negative weight cycle.
bellmanFord.realWorldUses_0: Distance-vector routing protocols (RIP, Routing Information Protocol)
bellmanFord.realWorldUses_1: Currency arbitrage detection (negative cycles in exchange rate graphs)
bellmanFord.realWorldUses_2: Network topology analysis with costs that can go negative (e.g., subsidies)
bellmanFord.facts_0: Bellman-Ford works with negative edge weights, unlike Dijkstra.
bellmanFord.facts_1: Its O(VE) complexity makes it slower than Dijkstra but more general.
bellmanFord.facts_2: Bellman-Ford can detect negative-weight cycles. No shortest path exists in their presence.

idaStar.history: IDA* (Iterative Deepening A*) was invented by Richard Korf in 1985 as a memory-efficient alternative to A*. While A* stores all generated nodes in memory, IDA* uses depth-first search with iteratively increasing cost thresholds, requiring only O(d) memory.
idaStar.intuition: Perform a depth-first search but with a cost threshold. Start with the heuristic value of the root as the threshold. Explore paths whose total cost f(n) = g(n) + h(n) does not exceed the threshold. If the goal is not found, increase the threshold to the minimum cost that exceeded it in the last iteration. Repeat.
idaStar.realWorldUses_0: Solving puzzles with large state spaces (15-puzzle, Rubik's Cube)
idaStar.realWorldUses_1: AI planning problems where memory is limited
idaStar.realWorldUses_2: Game tree search where breadth-first memory usage is prohibitive
idaStar.facts_0: IDA* uses O(d) memory, linear in the solution depth, versus A*'s exponential memory usage.
idaStar.facts_1: Like A*, IDA* is optimal and complete given an admissible heuristic.
idaStar.facts_2: The trade-off is time: IDA* may re-expand nodes many times, unlike A* which caches visited states.

dStarLite.history: D* Lite was developed by Sven Koenig and Maxim Likhachev in 2002 as a simplified and improved version of the original D* algorithm by Anthony Stentz (1994). It is designed for dynamic environments where the graph changes as the agent moves, exactly the challenge robots face in real-world navigation.
dStarLite.intuition: D* Lite plans a path from start to goal (like A*). As the robot moves and discovers new obstacles or changed costs, it efficiently updates the existing plan rather than replanning from scratch. It maintains consistency information about which parts of the plan are still valid, and only re-expands the minimum set of nodes needed after each change.
dStarLite.realWorldUses_0: NASA Mars rover navigation (Spirit and Opportunity used D* family algorithms)
dStarLite.realWorldUses_1: Autonomous ground robot navigation in dynamic environments
dStarLite.realWorldUses_2: Unknown terrain exploration with incremental map building
dStarLite.facts_0: D* Lite was used on NASA Mars rovers for real mission navigation.
dStarLite.facts_1: The 'Lite' in D* Lite means it is simpler to implement and understand than original D*.
dStarLite.facts_2: It is significantly more efficient than replanning from scratch on every change.

linearSearch.history: Sequential search is as old as ordered lists: scan from the beginning until you find a match or run out of elements. Every general-purpose language offers it as the baseline pattern before introducing binary search on sorted data.
linearSearch.intuition: Start at index 0. If that element equals the target, stop. Otherwise move to index 1, then 2, and so on. In the worst case you inspect every position once — O(n) time and O(1) extra space. If the target is at the front, you stop immediately — best case O(1).
linearSearch.realWorldUses_0: Small arrays or lists where setup cost of sorting or indexing is not justified
linearSearch.realWorldUses_1: Unsorted or singly-linked structures where you only have forward traversal
linearSearch.realWorldUses_2: Teaching why sorted-array tricks (binary search) buy logarithmic comparisons
linearSearch.facts_0: Linear search works on any order; Bayan Flow still generates sorted arrays in Searching mode for fair comparison with other algorithms.
linearSearch.facts_1: Worst-case comparisons grow linearly with n; average is still Θ(n) if the target is equally likely at any index.
linearSearch.facts_2: It is the simplest way to verify whether an element exists when you cannot assume sorted order.

binarySearch.history: Halving a sorted search space appears in very early computing. John Mauchly described a binary-search-style procedure for ordered tape in 1946. Once random-access memory and sorted tables were common, the iterative two-pointer form became the standard textbook algorithm.
binarySearch.intuition: Maintain a window [left, right] that might still hold the target. Compare the target with the middle element. If they match, stop. If the target is larger, drop the left half; if smaller, drop the right half. Each step removes about half of the remaining indices, so you need at most about log₂(n) comparisons.
binarySearch.realWorldUses_0: Lookups against sorted database indexes and ordered key ranges
binarySearch.realWorldUses_1: Standard library helpers (for example Python's bisect, C++ lower_bound)
binarySearch.realWorldUses_2: Finding insertion positions to keep arrays or lists sorted after updates
binarySearch.realWorldUses_3: Games, simulations, and APIs that query ordered numeric thresholds
binarySearch.facts_0: Binary search is O(log n) time and O(1) extra space for the classic iterative version.
binarySearch.facts_1: It only works on sorted input (or any total order where “middle” comparisons are valid).
binarySearch.facts_2: Integer overflow in mid = (left + right) / 2 is a famous pitfall; use left + (right - left) // 2 in languages where needed.
binarySearch.facts_3: Jon Bentley noted that many programmers still struggle to write a correct binary search on the first try.

ternarySearch.history: Partitioning a sorted range with more than one probe per iteration is a standard teaching step between linear scan and binary search. The two-probe, three-way split on arrays appears in many textbooks as “ternary search” alongside the classic unimodal optimization version on continuous domains.
ternarySearch.intuition: Keep a window [left, right] that may still contain the target. Place two indices near one-third and two-thirds of that window, compare the target with both values, then discard the third of the range that cannot contain the target (or the middle third when the target lies between the two probes). Each iteration uses two comparisons; the window still shrinks logarithmically, so the algorithm remains O(log n) in the worst case.
ternarySearch.realWorldUses_0: Teaching how two pivots divide the search interval into three visible segments
ternarySearch.realWorldUses_1: Visual contrast with binary search’s single midpoint on the same sorted-array model
ternarySearch.realWorldUses_2: Preparing for continuous-domain ternary search on unimodal functions (different problem, same split idea)
ternarySearch.realWorldUses_3: Discussions of comparison counts versus asymptotic class (two comparisons per step vs one in binary)
ternarySearch.facts_0: Ternary search on a sorted array is O(log n) time and O(1) extra space, same asymptotic class as binary search.
ternarySearch.facts_1: It requires sorted (ascending) input so ordering comparisons at the two probes are meaningful.
ternarySearch.facts_2: Each iteration compares the target with elements at two indices (roughly at ⌊(right−left)/3⌋ offsets), not one middle index.
ternarySearch.facts_3: Binary search usually wins on comparison count; ternary search highlights a different partition of the same ordered search space.

jumpSearch.history: Jump search is a classic block-based search on sorted arrays, combining coarse jumps with a short linear finish. It is often taught alongside binary search to compare √n versus logarithmic probe counts.
jumpSearch.intuition: Jump forward in blocks of about √n indices and compare the end of each block to the target. When a block’s end is at least the target, stop jumping and scan forward one index at a time from that block’s start. You make O(√n) jumps in the worst case and at most √n extra steps in the final segment.
jumpSearch.realWorldUses_0: Teaching trade-offs between jump-based and halving searches on sorted data
jumpSearch.realWorldUses_1: Systems where sequential memory access after a coarse jump is cheaper than random probes
jumpSearch.realWorldUses_2: Building intuition for root-n algorithms before binary search on tape-like or block-oriented storage stories
jumpSearch.realWorldUses_3: Paired with binary search in curricula that emphasize complexity classes
jumpSearch.facts_0: Jump search runs in O(√n) time and O(1) extra space on a sorted array.
jumpSearch.facts_1: The jump step is usually ⌊√n⌋ so the number of jumps and the final linear scan stay balanced.
jumpSearch.facts_2: Binary search typically needs fewer comparisons; jump search highlights a different time–structure trade-off.
jumpSearch.facts_3: Like binary search, jump search requires the input to be sorted (or indexed in sorted order).

interpolationSearch.history: Interpolation search was published by W. W. Peterson in 1957 as a way to exploit uniform key spacing in sorted tables. It remains a standard teaching example for how distribution assumptions change average-case cost.
interpolationSearch.intuition: Instead of probing the middle index, estimate where the target would sit if values increased evenly between the current bounds. Map the target’s rank in value-space to an index, clamp it, compare there, then shrink the range. When keys are roughly uniform, successive probes close in very quickly; when values clump or gaps are uneven, estimates can mislead and the method may approach linear behavior.
interpolationSearch.realWorldUses_0: Ordered numeric indexes and static tables where keys are spread somewhat evenly
interpolationSearch.realWorldUses_1: Contrasting midpoint search with value-based probing in CS curricula
interpolationSearch.realWorldUses_2: Building intuition for average O(log log n) versus worst-case O(n) on the same sorted-array interface
interpolationSearch.realWorldUses_3: Precursor ideas to extrapolation and interpolation in database and index structures
interpolationSearch.facts_0: Average-case time can be about O(log log n) when keys are uniformly distributed; worst case is O(n).
interpolationSearch.facts_1: The probe index uses both array values and indices, unlike binary search’s pure index halving.
interpolationSearch.facts_2: Duplicate or clustered values make interpolation less reliable; binary search stays O(log n) regardless of distribution.
interpolationSearch.facts_3: Guards for equal endpoints (arr[lo] === arr[hi]) avoid dividing by zero before comparing.

exponentialSearch.history: Exponential search (also called doubling search) is a standard two-phase algorithm for sorted data: find a bracketing range by probing indices that grow as powers of two, then finish with binary search within that range. It appears in textbooks on unbounded search and as a building block when the array length is unknown or effectively infinite.
exponentialSearch.intuition: First, jump forward so your index doubles each time (1, 2, 4, 8, …) until the value at that index is at least the target or you reach the end of the array. That brackets the target in O(log i) probes when the answer lies near index i. Then run ordinary binary search on the bounded interval — another logarithmic number of steps in that window.
exponentialSearch.realWorldUses_0: Searching sorted streams or lists where the length is unknown or very large
exponentialSearch.realWorldUses_1: Teaching how bracketing plus halving combines two logarithmic ideas
exponentialSearch.realWorldUses_2: Contrasting geometric growth with fixed-step jump search on the same sorted-array model
exponentialSearch.realWorldUses_3: Foundations for galloping variants in merge algorithms and adaptive search contexts
exponentialSearch.facts_0: On unbounded sorted data, finding a target at index i takes O(log i) comparisons in the doubling phase plus O(log i) for binary search on the bracket — O(log i) total.
exponentialSearch.facts_1: On a finite array of length n, the worst-case cost is still O(log n), like binary search, with an extra doubling phase before the first halving step.
exponentialSearch.facts_2: The algorithm requires sorted (ascending) values so comparisons tell you which side of a probe to keep.
exponentialSearch.facts_3: After doubling, the search interval is [⌊bound/2⌋, min(bound, n−1)] before binary search narrows it further.

fibonacciSearch.history: Fibonacci search uses Fibonacci numbers to choose probe positions on a sorted array, avoiding division in the inner loop. It is a classic teaching example alongside binary search when discussing golden-ratio splits and comparison-only search.
fibonacciSearch.intuition: Build the smallest Fibonacci number at least n+1 to size the first window. Each probe is at offset plus the second-smallest Fibonacci in the current triple. If the value is too small, slide the window right; if too large, shrink the Fibonacci triple so the remaining range is smaller. The split ratio follows the Fibonacci recurrence, so the window shrinks logarithmically.
fibonacciSearch.realWorldUses_0: Teaching division-free search and contrasting Fibonacci-based splits with midpoint binary search
fibonacciSearch.realWorldUses_1: Discussing historical cost models where division was expensive compared to addition and comparison
fibonacciSearch.realWorldUses_2: Reinforcing that O(log n) search does not require halving the index range
fibonacciSearch.realWorldUses_3: Pairing with number-theory topics (Fibonacci sequence, golden ratio) in CS curricula
fibonacciSearch.facts_0: Fibonacci search uses O(log n) comparisons in the worst case on a sorted array of length n, similar in class to binary search.
fibonacciSearch.facts_1: Probe index uses addition (offset + Fibonacci offset) rather than (left + right) / 2.
fibonacciSearch.facts_2: The active window length is tracked with Fibonacci triples (fibM, fibM−1, fibM−2) that shrink after each comparison.
fibonacciSearch.facts_3: Like binary search, the input must be sorted so ordering comparisons are meaningful.

depthFirstSearch.history: Depth-first search is a fundamental graph traversal strategy: explore as far as possible along one branch before backtracking. With an explicit stack on a node–link graph, it contrasts cleanly with breadth-first search in the same Searching category.
depthFirstSearch.intuition: Start at the root. Repeatedly pop the stack, visit that vertex, then push undiscovered neighbors in reverse adjacency order so the first listed neighbor is explored next. When the goal is popped, reconstruct one path using parent pointers. The path is valid but not necessarily shortest.
depthFirstSearch.realWorldUses_0: Teaching stack-based traversal on explicit vertices and edges
depthFirstSearch.realWorldUses_1: Trees and general graphs before introducing weights or heuristics
depthFirstSearch.realWorldUses_2: Contrasting traversal order with BFS — same graph, different data structure, different path
depthFirstSearch.realWorldUses_3: Foundations for topological reasoning and recursive algorithms expressed iteratively
depthFirstSearch.facts_0: Time is O(V + E) and extra space is O(V) for the stack and bookkeeping on V vertices and E edges.
depthFirstSearch.facts_1: Unlike BFS on an unweighted graph, DFS does not guarantee a shortest path to the goal.
depthFirstSearch.facts_2: Generated demos use random trees with sorted adjacency lists for reproducible DFS order.
depthFirstSearch.facts_3: Positions are layout-only; the algorithm uses the adjacency structure only.

breadthFirstSearchGraph.history: Breadth-first search explores a graph level by level, discovered by Konrad Zuse in 1945 and reinvented by Edward F. Moore in 1959. On unweighted graphs it guarantees the shortest path — the key distinction from DFS in this same Searching category.
breadthFirstSearchGraph.intuition: Start at the root and enqueue it. Repeatedly dequeue the front node, mark it visited, then enqueue all undiscovered neighbors. Because nodes are processed in the order they were discovered, BFS visits the entire depth-1 ring before any depth-2 node — guaranteeing a minimum-hop path to the goal.
breadthFirstSearchGraph.realWorldUses_0: Shortest-path on unweighted graphs (social networks, peer connections)
breadthFirstSearchGraph.realWorldUses_1: Level-order tree traversal and layer-by-layer graph analysis
breadthFirstSearchGraph.realWorldUses_2: Contrasting with DFS: same graph, queue vs. stack, shortest vs. any path
breadthFirstSearchGraph.realWorldUses_3: Foundation for Dijkstra's algorithm when edge weights become non-uniform
breadthFirstSearchGraph.facts_0: Time is O(V + E); space is O(V) for the queue and visited set.
breadthFirstSearchGraph.facts_1: BFS guarantees a shortest (minimum-hop) path on unweighted graphs; DFS does not.
breadthFirstSearchGraph.facts_2: The queue front is shown at the top of the canvas — the next node to be dequeued.
breadthFirstSearchGraph.facts_3: The same tree layout used for DFS is reused here, so you can compare both algorithms side by side on re-generation.

topologicalSort.history: Topological sorting grew from early work on partial orders and dependency graphs. The DFS completion-stack formulation is now a standard companion to depth-first search in algorithms courses and build-system discussions.
topologicalSort.intuition: A vertex can appear in the final order only after every vertex reachable through its outgoing edges has been handled. DFS naturally gives this: when a call finishes, push that vertex to a stack. Reversing the finish stack puts prerequisites before dependents.
topologicalSort.realWorldUses_0: Build systems that compile dependencies before dependents
topologicalSort.realWorldUses_3: Scheduling tasks with one-way prerequisite edges
topologicalSort.facts_0: A topological ordering exists if and only if the directed graph is acyclic.
topologicalSort.facts_1: Multiple valid orders may exist for the same DAG.
topologicalSort.facts_2: DFS temporary marks detect back edges, which prove a cycle.
topologicalSort.facts_3: The algorithm runs in O(V + E) time because each vertex and edge is processed once.

kahnAlgorithm.history: Arthur B. Kahn published this queue-based topological sorting method in 1962. It became the classic iterative counterpart to DFS topological sort because it frames the same problem through in-degrees instead of recursion and finish times.
kahnAlgorithm.intuition: Count how many incoming prerequisites each vertex still has. Any vertex with in-degree 0 is ready now, so put it in a queue. Remove one such vertex at a time, subtract its outgoing edges from neighbors, and enqueue every neighbor whose in-degree drops to 0.
kahnAlgorithm.realWorldUses_0: Dependency resolution with explicit cycle detection
kahnAlgorithm.realWorldUses_1: Task scheduling where ready work should be surfaced immediately
kahnAlgorithm.realWorldUses_2: Build graph validation before compilation starts
kahnAlgorithm.facts_0: If the queue becomes empty before every vertex is output, some cycle still supplies incoming edges.
kahnAlgorithm.facts_1: The algorithm is O(V + E) because each vertex is queued once and each edge is removed once.
kahnAlgorithm.facts_2: Different queue tie-break rules can produce different valid topological orders.
kahnAlgorithm.facts_3: This implementation keeps the ready queue sorted for deterministic educational output.

inorderTraversal.history: Systematic binary-tree walks were codified early in compiler construction and compiler textbooks: visit left subtree, node, right subtree (inorder). When the structure is a binary search tree, inorder yields keys in sorted order — the bridge between pointer structure and ordering.
inorderTraversal.intuition: Starting from the root, push along left children until there is none; then visit (record) the node, then move to its right child and repeat. Every node is visited exactly once after its entire left subtree has been processed—this is why BST inorder prints sorted keys.
inorderTraversal.realWorldUses_0: BST range queries and sorted enumeration without maintaining a separate sorted array
inorderTraversal.realWorldUses_1: Recovering infix notation from expression trees
inorderTraversal.realWorldUses_2: Teaching depth-first patterns before preorder and postorder
inorderTraversal.realWorldUses_3: Foundation for Euler tours and tree iterators in standard libraries
inorderTraversal.facts_0: Time is Θ(n) on n nodes; extra space is O(h) for an explicit stack with tree height h.
inorderTraversal.facts_1: Random trees here assign values by inorder indexing so inorder produces 1…n — a valid BST.
inorderTraversal.facts_2: Recursive formulation matches the iterative stack behavior shown step by step.
inorderTraversal.facts_3: Unlike graph DFS with a goal, traversal visits every node exactly once.

levelOrderTraversal.history: Level-order traversal is breadth-first search specialized to trees: process the root, then every node one edge away, then every node two edges away, and so on. As a tree algorithm it is the queue-based counterpoint to the stack/recursion style used by inorder, preorder, and postorder.
levelOrderTraversal.intuition: Start by enqueuing the root. Repeatedly dequeue the front node, visit it, then enqueue its children from left to right. Because the queue preserves discovery order, all nodes at depth d are processed before any node at depth d + 1.
levelOrderTraversal.realWorldUses_0: Tree serialization formats that need level-by-level output
levelOrderTraversal.realWorldUses_1: Finding the nearest node that matches a condition
levelOrderTraversal.realWorldUses_2: Printing or analyzing trees by layers instead of by branches
levelOrderTraversal.realWorldUses_3: Teaching queue-based breadth-first thinking before moving to graph BFS
levelOrderTraversal.facts_0: Time is Θ(n) on n nodes; auxiliary space is O(w), where w is the maximum width of the tree.
levelOrderTraversal.facts_1: Unlike the depth-first traversals, level-order depends on a queue rather than a stack or recursion.
levelOrderTraversal.facts_2: On the same tree shape, level-order highlights horizontal layers rather than root-to-leaf depth.
levelOrderTraversal.facts_3: This traversal is also the standard BFS pattern on trees because each edge has equal weight.

zigzagLevelOrderTraversal.history: Zigzag (spiral) level-order is breadth-first layering with an interview-favorite twist: within each depth, the visit direction alternates. It appears in textbooks and contests as “binary tree zigzag traversal” layered on ordinary BFS batching.
zigzagLevelOrderTraversal.intuition: Like level-order, gather all nodes of the next depth together. Snapshot them in spatial left-to-right order for building the queue, but visit odd depths in reverse to create the zigzag effect. Toggle a single Boolean (or parity) instead of rewriting the breadth-first scaffold.
zigzagLevelOrderTraversal.realWorldUses_0: LeetCode-style tree traversal exercises and directional pretty-print layouts
zigzagLevelOrderTraversal.realWorldUses_1: Contrasting vanilla level-order with a tiny state-machine change
zigzagLevelOrderTraversal.realWorldUses_2: Teaching deque / reverse patterns while keeping overall BFS guarantees
zigzagLevelOrderTraversal.realWorldUses_3: Serialization puzzles that require alternating row direction
zigzagLevelOrderTraversal.facts_0: Still Θ(n) time; space remains O(w) for the widest level when you enqueue one layer at a time.
zigzagLevelOrderTraversal.facts_1: The zigzag permutation does not reorder children for the next level—spatial left-to-right must be preserved.
zigzagLevelOrderTraversal.facts_2: The breadcrumb badges show scan direction separately from remaining frontier nodes.
zigzagLevelOrderTraversal.facts_3: Odd-sized levels still zigzag—you simply reverse single-node rows with no visible change.

preorderTraversal.history: Preorder (prefix) order—node, then left subtree, then right subtree—is standard in textbooks on trees and expression evaluation. Together with inorder it is one half of the classic pair used to reconstruct a binary tree when node values are unique.
preorderTraversal.intuition: Always record the current node before going deeper: visit the node, then explore the entire left branch, then the entire right branch. The visualization uses an explicit stack (push right before left so left is processed first), mirroring recursive calls without unbounded recursion depth in the UI runner.
preorderTraversal.realWorldUses_0: Copying or serializing a tree structure (save node, then children)
preorderTraversal.realWorldUses_2: Tree reconstruction when combined with inorder traversal
preorderTraversal.realWorldUses_3: Teaching parent-first depth-first thinking before postorder
preorderTraversal.facts_0: Time is Θ(n) on n nodes; extra space is O(h) for the explicit stack with tree height h.
preorderTraversal.facts_1: The same random binary trees as inorder use BST-shaped labels (inorder yields 1…n); preorder order depends on shape, not sorted labels.
preorderTraversal.facts_2: The Python panel includes both recursive and iterative implementations with identical visit order.
preorderTraversal.facts_3: Preorder alone does not yield sorted keys on a BST—inorder does.

postorderTraversal.history: Postorder (suffix/postfix) order—left subtree, right subtree, then node—is the third member of the classic traversal trio. It is the natural order for recursive tree deletion and dependency resolution, where you must finish children before handling the parent.
postorderTraversal.intuition: Process both children entirely before handling the current node: descend left, then right, then visit. Unlike preorder's 'node-first' approach, postorder processes leaf nodes before their ancestors. This matches deletion logic (delete subtrees before deleting the node) and topological sorting of DAGs.
postorderTraversal.realWorldUses_0: Deleting or destroying a tree structure (delete children before parent, avoiding dangling pointers)
postorderTraversal.realWorldUses_1: Computing tree height, size, or aggregate values bottom-up
postorderTraversal.realWorldUses_2: Postfix (Reverse Polish) notation and expression evaluation
postorderTraversal.realWorldUses_3: Dependency resolution in build systems and task scheduling (tasks done before their dependents)
postorderTraversal.facts_0: Time is Θ(n) on n nodes; extra space is O(h) for the explicit stack with tree height h.
postorderTraversal.facts_1: Two-stack iterative approach: first stack processes nodes; second stack collects result in reverse postorder.
postorderTraversal.facts_2: Recursively matches the iterative version: recurse left, recurse right, then visit.
postorderTraversal.facts_3: Postorder + inorder uniquely determine a tree (assuming unique node values); postorder alone does not.

morrisTraversal.history: Joseph M. Morris published the threaded inorder walk in 1979—an elegant answer to “inorder without recursion or a stack.” Temporary right-child links (threads) encode the same push/pop information a stack would hold, then the tree is repaired in place.
morrisTraversal.intuition: For each node with a left child, find its inorder predecessor (rightmost in the left subtree). If that predecessor’s right is null, wire it to the current node as a return link and go left. If the right already points back to the current node, you’ve finished the left subtree—remove the wire, visit the node, go right. No extra structures—only clever pointer surgery.
morrisTraversal.realWorldUses_0: Interview and exam questions on space-bounded tree algorithms
morrisTraversal.realWorldUses_1: Very small embedded systems where stack depth is risky or unavailable
morrisTraversal.realWorldUses_2: Teaching how tree structure can double as navigation state
morrisTraversal.realWorldUses_3: Contrasting with iterative inorder that needs O(h) stack space
morrisTraversal.facts_0: Time Θ(n): each real edge is crossed a constant number of times; same visit order as classic inorder.
morrisTraversal.facts_1: Extra space is O(1) pointers only—no recursion stack, no explicit stack, no queue.
morrisTraversal.facts_2: Threads are removed before the walk ends; the tree ends with the same shape it started with.
morrisTraversal.facts_3: The visualization draws each temporary thread as an extra edge for that step.

footer.description: An interactive, educational web application for visualizing sorting, searching, and pathfinding algorithms with clarity.
footer.enjoying: Enjoying this project? Support my work!
footer.youtubeAria: Bayan Flow on YouTube — open channel in a new tab

algorithmSteps.bubblePassComplete: Pass {{pass}} complete. Element at position {{position}} is in correct position.
algorithmSteps.bubbleEarlyTermination: Array is sorted! Early termination after {{pass}} passes (saved {{totalPasses}} total passes).
algorithmSteps.dfsStackPush: Pushed neighbors onto the stack; stack size is {{count}} (next vertex is popped depth-first)
algorithmSteps.bfsGraphDequeue: Dequeued node {{node}} — examining neighbors level by level
algorithmSteps.bfsGraphEnqueue: Enqueued {{count}} neighbor(s); queue front is next
algorithmSteps.inorderStart: Inorder traversal: left subtree, node, right subtree (LNR).
algorithmSteps.inorderEmpty: Tree is empty — nothing to traverse.
algorithmSteps.levelOrderStart: Level-order traversal: visit the tree one layer at a time using a queue.
algorithmSteps.levelOrderEmpty: Tree is empty — nothing to traverse.
algorithmSteps.zigzagLevelStart: Zigzag level-order: same layers as BFS, but scan left-to-right on even depths and right-to-left on odd depths (starting at the root).
algorithmSteps.zigzagLevelEmpty: Tree is empty — nothing to traverse.
algorithmSteps.preorderStart: Preorder traversal: node, then left subtree, then right subtree (NLR).
algorithmSteps.preorderEmpty: Tree is empty — nothing to traverse.
algorithmSteps.postorderStart: Postorder traversal: left subtree, then right subtree, then node (LRN).
algorithmSteps.postorderEmpty: Tree is empty — nothing to traverse.
algorithmSteps.morrisStart: Morris traversal: inorder (LNR) with O(1) extra space — temporary threads replace a stack.
algorithmSteps.morrisThreading: Thread: link predecessor {{from}} → {{to}} so we can return after the left subtree.
algorithmSteps.morrisUnthreading: Unthread: restore {{from}}'s right pointer; left subtree of {{to}} is done — visit {{to}}.
algorithmSteps.morrisEmpty: Tree is empty — nothing to traverse.
algorithmSteps.topologicalStart: Topological sort: run DFS and record each vertex after all outgoing edges finish.
algorithmSteps.topologicalEnter: Enter {{node}} and add it to the recursion stack.
algorithmSteps.topologicalCompleteNode: All outgoing edges from {{node}} are done. Push it onto the finish stack: {{stack}}.
algorithmSteps.topologicalCycle: Cycle detected on edge {{from}} → {{to}}. A topological order only exists for DAGs.
algorithmSteps.topologicalFinish: Reverse the finish stack to get a valid topological order: {{order}}.
algorithmSteps.kahnStart: Kahn's algorithm: compute in-degrees and enqueue every vertex with in-degree 0. Initial queue: {{queue}}.
algorithmSteps.kahnDequeue: Dequeue {{node}} and append it to the topological order. Order so far: {{order}}.
algorithmSteps.kahnRelaxEdge: Remove edge {{from}} → {{to}}. The in-degree of {{to}} drops to {{inDegree}}.
algorithmSteps.kahnCycle: The queue is empty after ordering {{count}} of {{total}} vertices. Remaining incoming edges prove a cycle.
algorithmSteps.kahnFinish: All vertices were removed successfully. Final topological order: {{order}}.
algorithmSteps.pathFoundGraph: Path found! {{length}} nodes on the discovery path
algorithmSteps.bidirectionalProgress: {{direction}} search progress - Forward queue: {{forwardQueue}}, Backward queue: {{backwardQueue}}
algorithmSteps.bidirectionalMeeting: Searches met at ({{row}}, {{col}})! Path length: {{pathLength}} cells
algorithmSteps.jpsJumpPointAt: Jump point at ({{row}}, {{col}}) | g={{g}}, h={{h}}, f={{f}}
algorithmSteps.jpsJumpPointsDiscovered: Discovered {{count}} jump point(s) in open set
algorithmSteps.selectionFindingMin: Finding minimum in unsorted region starting at position {{position}}
algorithmSteps.insertionKey: Selecting {{value}} at position {{position}} to insert into sorted portion
algorithmSteps.insertionPassComplete: Pass {{pass}} complete. First {{elements}} elements are now sorted.
algorithmSteps.shellGap: Using Knuth's gap sequence with gap size: {{gap}} (comparing elements {{gap}} positions apart)
algorithmSteps.shellSelecting: Selecting element {{value}} at position {{position}} for gap-{{gap}} insertion sort
algorithmSteps.shellComparing: Comparing {{a}} (pos {{posA}}) with {{b}} across gap of {{gap}} positions
algorithmSteps.shellPlaced: Placed {{value}} at its correct position {{position}} for this gap
algorithmSteps.shellGapComplete: Completed gap-{{gap}} pass. Array is now {{gap}}-sorted, moving to smaller gap.
algorithmSteps.combGap: Using gap size: {{gap}} (comparing elements {{gap}} positions apart)
algorithmSteps.combGapComplete: Completed gap-{{gap}} pass. Moving to smaller gap.
algorithmSteps.timRunSorted: Run sorted from position {{start}} to {{end}} using insertion sort
algorithmSteps.countingFindMax: Found maximum value: {{max}}. Creating counting array of size {{max}} + 1.
algorithmSteps.countingCountComplete: Counting phase complete. Each value's occurrences have been counted.
algorithmSteps.countingCumulativeComplete: Cumulative sum calculated. Count array now contains positions.
algorithmSteps.countingPlace: Placing {{value}} at position {{position}} in output array
algorithmSteps.cycleStart: Starting cycle with item {{item}} at position {{position}}
algorithmSteps.cycleSkip: Item {{item}} is already in correct position
algorithmSteps.bellmanNoUpdates: No updates in this iteration. Algorithm terminated early.
algorithmSteps.bogoChecking: Attempt {{attempt}}: Checking if array is sorted
algorithmSteps.bogoSuccess: Success! Array sorted after {{attempts}} shuffle attempts
algorithmSteps.bogoFailed: Failed to sort after {{maxAttempts}} attempts. Bogo Sort is extremely inefficient!
algorithmSteps.searchLinearStart: Searching for {{target}} with linear search: check each index from left to right in order. This mode uses a sorted array so you can compare with binary and other searches; linear search itself does not require sorting.
algorithmSteps.searchLinearNotFound: Target {{target}} is not in the array.
algorithmSteps.searchBinaryStart: Searching for {{target}} using binary search on the sorted array.
algorithmSteps.searchBinaryCheck: Middle index {{mid}}: value {{value}}. Active range [{{left}}, {{right}}]. Target {{target}}.
algorithmSteps.searchBinaryNotFound: Target {{target}} is not in the array.
algorithmSteps.searchTernaryStart: Searching for {{target}} using ternary search on the sorted array (two probes split the active range into three parts).
algorithmSteps.searchTernaryCheck: Left third index {{leftMid}}: value {{leftValue}}. Right third index {{rightMid}}: value {{rightValue}}. Active range [{{left}}, {{right}}]. Target {{target}}.
algorithmSteps.searchTernaryNotFound: Target {{target}} is not in the array.
algorithmSteps.searchJumpStart: Searching for {{target}} using jump search on the sorted array. Block step size {{m}} (⌊√n⌋).
algorithmSteps.searchJumpBlockCheck: Jump phase — block start {{prev}}, block end index {{jumpIdx}}: value {{value}}. Step size {{m}}. Target {{target}}.
algorithmSteps.searchJumpLinearCheck: Linear scan at index {{prev}}: value {{value}}. Target {{target}}.
algorithmSteps.searchJumpNotFound: Target {{target}} is not in the array.
algorithmSteps.searchInterpolationStart: Searching for {{target}} using interpolation search. Probes estimate index from values at the bounds, not the midpoint.
algorithmSteps.searchInterpolationProbe: Probe index {{pos}} (from values at {{lo}}..{{hi}}: {{loVal}} .. {{hiVal}}): value {{value}}. Active range [{{lo}}, {{hi}}]. Target {{target}}.
algorithmSteps.searchInterpolationNotFound: Target {{target}} is not in the array.
algorithmSteps.searchExponentialStart: Searching for {{target}} using exponential search on the sorted array.
algorithmSteps.searchExponentialCheckFirst: Doubling phase — compare index 0: value {{value}}. Target {{target}}.
algorithmSteps.searchExponentialDoubledProbe: Doubling phase — compare index {{bound}}: value {{value}}. Target {{target}}. Next bound would be {{nextBound}} (until the range is found).
algorithmSteps.searchExponentialRangeLocked: Range bracketed: if {{target}} exists, it lies between indices {{left}} and {{right}}. Binary search phase — narrow this window.
algorithmSteps.searchExponentialBinaryCheck: Binary phase — middle index {{mid}}: value {{value}}. Active range [{{left}}, {{right}}]. Target {{target}}.
algorithmSteps.searchExponentialNotFound: Target {{target}} is not in the array.
algorithmSteps.searchFibonacciStart: Searching for {{target}} using Fibonacci search on the sorted array (probe positions follow Fibonacci-sized steps — no division).
algorithmSteps.searchFibonacciProbe: Probe index {{probe}}: value {{value}}. Active window [{{offset}}, {{windowEnd}}]. Target {{target}}.
algorithmSteps.searchFibonacciNotFound: Target {{target}} is not in the array.

hero.title: Finally, Algorithms Make Sense
hero.subtitle: Watch algorithms come to life with interactive, step-by-step visualizations. Learn at your own pace.

learnYourWay.heading: Learn Your Way

autoPlay.title: Auto-Play Mode
autoPlay.description: Sit back and watch algorithms unfold automatically at your preferred speed. Perfect for understanding the big picture.

manual.title: Manual Control
manual.description: Step through each operation manually. Pause, rewind, and analyze every decision the algorithm makes.

learnYourWay.tagline: You decide how fast you learn.

clarity.heading: No Clutter, Just Clarity
clarity.description: Bayan Flow strips away complexity to reveal the beauty of algorithms. Clean interface, clear explanations, and vivid animations help you see what's really happening.

algorithmTypes.heading: Explore Algorithm Types
algorithmTypes.subheading: Dive into different algorithm categories and see how they work step by step

sorting.title: Sorting Algorithms
sorting.description: Watch how different sorting algorithms organize data step by step. Perfect for understanding comparison-based sorting.
sorting.algorithms: Bubble Sort, Quick Sort, Merge Sort

pathfinding.title: Pathfinding Algorithms
pathfinding.description: See how pathfinding algorithms navigate through grids to find the shortest path between two points.
pathfinding.algorithms: BFS, Dijkstra, A* Search, Bidirectional Search, Greedy Best-First Search, Jump Point Search, Bellman-Ford, IDA*, D* Lite

searching.title: Searching Algorithms
searching.description: Follow lookups on sorted data step by step. See how each probe narrows the range until the target is found or ruled out.
searching.algorithms: Binary Search, Jump Search, Interpolation Search, Exponential Search

treeTraversal.title: Tree Traversals
treeTraversal.description: Explore classic tree traversal orders and learn how each visit order explores a tree structure.
treeTraversal.algorithms: Inorder Traversal, Level-order Traversal, Zigzag Level-order Traversal, Preorder Traversal, Postorder Traversal, Morris Traversal

graphAlgorithm.title: Graph Algorithms
graphAlgorithm.description: Study directed graph problems such as dependency ordering, starting with DFS-based topological sort on generated DAGs.

features.heading: Powerful Features
features.subheading: Everything you need to master algorithms

customization.title: Customizable
customization.description: Adjust array size (5-100) for sorting and searching, or grid size (15×15 to 35×35) for pathfinding, to see how algorithms perform with different data sizes.

pythonCode.title: Interactive Python
pythonCode.description: Edit, run, and see real output for each algorithm. All in your browser. Learn by experimenting with live code.

sound.title: Interactive Sound
sound.description: Enable audio feedback to hear algorithms in action.

fullscreen.title: Full Screen
fullscreen.description: Immersive full-screen mode perfect for presentations and classroom teaching.

insight.title: Algorithm Insight
insight.description: Explore detailed explanations, use cases, and key properties for each algorithm in a dedicated panel.

videoExport.title: Video Export
videoExport.description: Export your visualizations as HD MP4 videos. Choose horizontal (YouTube) or vertical (Shorts, Reels) format with preview before download.

roadmapCTA.heading: See What's Next
roadmapCTA.description: We're constantly improving Bayan Flow with new algorithms, features, and learning tools. Check out our roadmap to see what's coming and what we're working on right now.

hero.title: Where We're Going
hero.subtitle: Every update, every DevLog. See what's live and what's next.
hero.devNote: Want to see the latest changes and experiments? Check out our <0>dev version</0> and share your feedback by <1>creating an issue</1> on our GitHub repo.

timeline.sampleNote: Timeline items are samples. Replace with actual roadmap content.
